coredata.dc:description: Diclofenac (DCF) has received widespread attention due to its potential negative influence on the environment and human health. And the development of efficient technology for DCF removal becomes a global challenge. Herein, a novel visible-light-driven photocatalyst (PCN@mCQDs-2) was successfully synthesized using self-assembly melamine-cyanuric acid supermolecules regulated by mCQDs as precursors. mCQDs could involve in the process of supramolecular self-assembly through hydrogen bonding and show regulation effects on the microstructure control, band structure adjustment, and performance promotion of as-prepared PCN@mCQDs-2. Benefited from the multiple advantages, PCN@mCQDs-2 exhibited about 25 times higher degradation rate towards DCF than pure graphitic carbon nitride. Experimental results reflected that photogenerated holes dominated the degradation of DCF, while hydroxyl radical showed a negligible contribution. Further research indicated that PCN@mCQDs-2 has high toxicity reduction properties, mineralization ability, and reusability. Our findings provide a novel idea for the fabrication of enhanced g-C3N4 for environmental protection.

(b) Photocatalytic degradation of DCF over PCN@mCQDs-2 in five cycling runs.

head.ce:title: Self-assembled graphitic carbon nitride regulated by carbon quantum dots with optimized electronic band structure for enhanced photocatalytic degradation of diclofenac

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Diclofenac (DCF) has received widespread attention due to its potential negative influence on the environment and human health. And the development of efficient technology for DCF removal becomes a global challenge. Herein, a novel visible-light-driven photocatalyst (PCN@mCQDs-2) was successfully synthesized using self-assembly melamine-cyanuric acid supermolecules regulated by mCQDs as precursors. mCQDs could involve in the process of supramolecular self-assembly through hydrogen bonding and show regulation effects on the microstructure control, band structure adjustment, and performance promotion of as-prepared PCN@mCQDs-2. Benefited from the multiple advantages, PCN@mCQDs-2 exhibited about 25 times higher degradation rate towards DCF than pure graphitic carbon nitride. Experimental results reflected that photogenerated holes dominated the degradation of DCF, while hydroxyl radical showed a negligible contribution. Further research indicated that PCN@mCQDs-2 has high toxicity reduction properties, mineralization ability, and reusability. Our findings provide a novel idea for the fabrication of enhanced g-C3N4 for environmental protection.

ce:section.ce:para: Pharmaceutical and personal care products (PPCPs) have been widely detected in the environment due to their large amount of consumption and inefficient removal in conventional wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs) [1–4]. According to reports, PPCPs have a negative influence on the aquatic environment and human health because of their high toxicity, endocrine-disrupting effects, bioaccumulation capacity, and pseudo persistence [5–9]. Diclofenac (DCF) is a common anti-inflammatory drug with high toxicity and highest detection rates, added to the first European Union Water Framework Directive Watch List for monitoring and control [10–12]. Previous research has indicated that DCF could interfere with the growth, development, reproduction, genetics, endocrine, and metabolism of aquatic organisms and threaten human health in chronic exposure even at low environmental concentrations [13–17]. DCF has been reported to be not biodegradable and often be difficult to eliminate from water in WWTPs [18]. Therefore, developing effective and economical techniques to remove DCF from wastewater and environmental water has become a global challenge.

So far, several technologies have been used to trap or degrade DCF, such as adsorption, ozonation, photocatalytic degradation, Fenton-like technology, photo-electrocatalytic degradation, and bioremediation [18–23]. Among these methods, owing to the high degradation and even mineralization efficiency of recalcitrant pollutants and environmental friendliness, photocatalysis has been considered as one of the most promising techniques [24,25]. Graphite carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is a promising metal-free photocatalyst with a graphite-like structure, which has a suitable bandgap (2.7 eV), excellent chemical stability, large surface area, tunable electronic band structure, and excellent visible light response activity [26–29]. Composed of earth-abundant elements, g-C3N4 is an environmentally friendly material for low cost and no eco-toxicity. Consequently, g-C3N4-based photocatalyst has been widely used to remove PPCPs in aqueous solutions [30–32].

Considering the low electrical conductivity, long-distance diffusion, and fast recombination of photoinduced charge carriers of g-C3N4, serval modification methods have been applied to enhance the photocatalytic activity, including surface modification, element doping, defect engineering, morphology control, construction of heterojunctions, and supramolecular self-assembly [33–38]. Supramolecular is a kind of aggregate with ordered structure and morphology formed through the weak interactions of precursor molecules spontaneously. Supramolecular could be made into an excellent photocatalyst with more exposed active sites, narrower bandgap, higher electric conductivity, more efficient photogenerated carrier separation, and enhanced light absorption capabilities [39–41]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a type of carbon-based nanomaterial with no toxicity, high biocompatibility, the virtue of ease of preparation and modification, outstanding electron transport performance, and unique up-conversion luminescence properties [42,43]. To improve the carrier separation efficiency and broaden the light absorption region, CQDs have been widely utilized to integrate with semiconductors include g-C3N4 [24,44–46].

However, CQDs are usually directly loaded and introduced after g-C3N4 was prepared, without exploring the possible interaction between CQDs and the precursors of g-C3N4 [2,45,46]. Generally, CQDs are rich in abundant amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups, which might participate in the process of supramolecular self-assembly through hydrogen bonding [47]. The role of CQDs in the construction of self-assembled supramolecules and their regulation effects on the microstructure control, band structure adjustment, and performance promotion of as-prepared photocatalyst is not yet known, and the corresponding mechanism has not yet been reported. Therefore, we report the synthesis of modified carbon nitride with highly photocatalytic activity by facile polymerization, using a self-assembly melamine-cyanuric acid supermolecule regulated by CQDs as precursors. The obtained photocatalysts (PCN@mCQDs-2) were characterized, and the photocatalytic performance was investigated through degradation experiments of DCF as well as several other PPCPs. The photocatalytic degradation pathways and mechanisms of diclofenac were discussed, while the toxicity reduction, mineralization, and reusability of PCN@mCQDs-2 were also assessed. Hence, our results provide an idea for the fabrication of enhanced g-C3N4 for environmental protection.

Reagents, characterizations, photoelectrochemical measurements, analytical methods, acute toxicity tests, and reusability tests are provided in Text S1, S2, S3, S4, S5, and S6 in Supplementary Material.

Two types of CQDs were fabricated from the same raw materials via modified microwave and hydrothermal methods based on previous studies and recorded as mCQDs and hCQDs, respectively [45,48]. Typically, 16 mmol of citric acid and urea were firstly dissolved in 8 mL deionized (DI) water and vigorously stirred to form a transparent mixture solution. To synthesizing mCQDs, the mixture solution was transferred into a 700 W household microwave oven and heated for 10 mins. The solid product was suspended and then centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 30 mins. The solution was dried overnight at 70 °C to obtain the mCQDs powder. When preparing hCQDs, the mixture solution undergoes a hydrothermal process at 180 °C for 5 h. The obtained suspension was centrifuged and dried according to the above conditions to prepare the hCQDs powder. Finally, the obtained mCQDs and hCQDs powders were fully suspended to prepare the stock solutions (10 g/L) and then stocked at 4 °C for subsequent synthetic procedures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PCN, PCN@mCQDs-x, PCN@hCQDs-x and g-C3N4
ce:section.ce:para: The porous carbon nitride (PCN) was synthesized via a supramolecular self-assembly process based on previous studies [49]. Typically, 2.5224 g of melamine (20 mmol) and 2.5816 g of cyanuric acid (20 mmol) were dissolved in 100 mL of DI water and stirred for 12 h. Then, the obtained suspension was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 mins and frozen-drying (pre-frozen at − 80 °C for 4 h) for two days to obtain cyanuric acid-melamine supramolecular. Finally, the supramolecular precursors were calcined in a muffle furnace at 550 °C for 4 h.

As demonstrated in Scheme 1 , PCN@mCQDs-x and PCN@hCQDs-x (x = 1, 2, and 3, representing 0.5%, 1.0%, and 1.5% weight percentage of CQDs to PCN) were also fabricated according to the same method, except adding a certain quantity of mCQDs or hCQDs stock solutions (2.552, 5.104, and 7.656 mL when x = 1, 2, and 3) before stirring. As a comparison, the pure melamine was calcined in the same condition to synthesize the bulk g-C3N4. Besides, the MCN and mMCN-x (x = 1, 2, and 3) were prepared based on the methods of PCN and PCN@mCQDs-x except calcining. Moreover, mMCN-2-y (y = 4 or 8 h, representing the stirring time) were also prepared by changing the stirring time from 12 h to 4 or 8 h.

The photocatalytic degradation of DCF was exerted in a PCX-50C Discover Multi-channel photochemical reactor (Fig. S1, BeijingPerfect Light, Beijing, China) with a UV cutoff filter (λ ≥ 420 nm). The light intensity was detected to be 150 ± 5 mW/cm2 by an LH-122 Solar Power Meter. Unless otherwise stated, each quartz tube contains 8 mg/L DCF aqueous solution and 0.1 g/L catalysts. Then the pH was adjusted to the desired values, and the reaction temperature was maintained at 25.0 ± 0.1 °C by a DHX-1010 circulating water bath (Nanjing Fandilang Information Technology Co., Nanjing, China). The reaction solution was stirred at 300 rpm for 30 mins in darkness. Samples were taken and filtered regularly for subsequent analysis during the photocatalytic process. Each test was performed in triplicate.

Then, the extra degradation experiments of diclofenac were conducted according to the above conditions except for light sources. Precisely, five 9 W light-emitting diodes (LEDs; Epileds Technologies, Taiwan, China) were placed 5 cm away from the experimental solutions to generate blue light (455–460 nm), blue-green light (470–480 nm), green light (515–530 nm), orange light (600–605 nm), and red light (620–630 nm), and the light intensity of all was detected to be 56.2 ± 0.2, 55.1 ± 0.2, 36.9 ± 0.2, 20.6 ± 0.2, 21.3 ± 0.2 mW/cm2, respectively.

The contribution of different oxidative radicals generated in photocatalysis were evaluated by serval scavenger-quenching experiments and the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectrometer (JEOL JES-FA200). Detailed information is shown in Table S2.

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of all samples were investigated. Consistent with previous studies, pure g-C3N4 exhibited a block-like structure consisting of solid agglomerates in Fig. 1 a [50]. As shown in Fig. 1b, PCN formed by supramolecular self-assembly displayed an ultrathin hollow broken sheet structure. In contrast, the morphology of PCN@mCQDs-2 and PCN@hCQDs-2 changed significantly after adding a small amount of CQDs during the self-assembly process, revealing a hollow tubular structure and irregular structure with rich pores, respectively. It may be explained that mCQDs and hCQDs involved in the process of supramolecular self-assembly and showed the regulation effects on the microstructure of as-prepared photocatalysts. The HRTEM images of PCN@mCQDs-2 were investigated to further confirm the existence of mCQDs and their regulation effects. The images that contain lots of CQDs were not found, which may be because mCQDs deeply involve in supramolecules self-assembly rather than simply being loaded on the photocatalysts [51]. The distinct lattice fringes monitored to be 0.32 nm were observed in Fig. 1h and 1i, attributed to the graphite phase of mCQDs because the crystal structure of carbon nitride is more unstable under the electron beam irradiation [52].

The crystal phase structures and crystallinity of as-prepared photocatalysts were presented in the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns in Fig. 2 a. All samples showed two characteristic diffraction peaks of g-C3N4 (JCPDS No. 87–1526) at 2θ = 27.5° (002) and 13.0° (100), which are assigned to tri-s-triazine ring in-plane compression and interlayer-stacking structure of the conjugated aromatic system, respectively [53]. Compared to g-C3N4, two distinct diffraction peaks ((100) and (002)) of PCN decreased while no peak appeared, indicating that the crystallinity decreased while the graphite-like phase structure did not change [54]. After CQDs regulation, no diffraction peak for CQDs was observed, while the full width at half maxima of PCN@mCQDs-2 and PCN@hCQDs-2 were significantly improved, meaning increased crystallinity and shortened stacking distance [55]. Compared with g-C3N4, the diffraction peak at (002) for PCN, PCN@mCQDs-2, and PCN@hCQDs-2 exhibited a right shift from 27.40° to 27.54°, 27.60°, and 27.72°, corresponding to the interlayer spacing narrowing down from 0.325 to 0.323, 0.323 and 0.321 nm (calculated by 2dsinθ = nλ) [56]. The narrower interlayer distance usually means a faster charge transfer rate and improved photocatalytic performance [57]. Hence, consistent with the results above, CQDs might involve in the process of supramolecular self-assembly and affect crystallization.

The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) of as-prepared photocatalysts were conducted and illustrated in Fig. 2b. All samples displayed the typical stretching and bending vibrations of g-C3N4 at 3000–3400, 1000–1700, and 812 cm−1, corresponding to the vibration of O−H and N−H, the C−N stretching vibrations, and the vibration mode of the triazine ring [58,59]. PCN@mCQDs-2 and PCN@hCQDs-2 showed a similar framework to PCN, while PCN@mCQDs-2 exhibited the higher characteristic peaks as shown in Fig. 2c. This highlights that PCN@mCQDs-2 has a more ordered packing of the triazine units and more hydroxyl and amino groups [46,50,60]. The hydrogen bonding between melamine and cyanuric acid in self-assembly supramolecules was proved by the shifts of C=O stretching vibration of cyanuric acid (from 1691 to 1734 cm−1) and the triazine ring vibration of melamine (from 814 cm−1 to 769 cm−1) in FT-IR spectra [39,61,62]. As shown in Fig. 2d, with the regulation of mCQDs, the C=O stretching vibration and the triazine ring vibration of mMCN-x shifted to around 1705 and 813 cm−1, respectively, indicating that mCQDs affected the process of supramolecular self-assembly. Further, mMCN-x have a higher peak of C=O stretching vibration, indicative of increased self-assembly and more hydrogen bonding [63]. And the wavenumber shifts and stronger peak intensity of –NH2 and –NH stretching vibration are also assigned to the effect of hydrogen bonding [64]. In addition, Fig. S2a and Table S3 suggested mCQDs contained abundant amnio groups and oxygen-containing groups, and Fig. S2c presented the regulation of mCQDs on the supramolecules self-assembly enhanced with the increased hydrothermal time. The results above confirmed that mCQDs participated in supramolecular self-assembly through hydrogen bonding.

The surface areas and pore structure of all samples were studied via nitrogen adsorption experiments. As shown in isotherms of Fig. 3 a, compared with a faint hysteresis loop of g-C3N4, other samples exhibited type IV isotherms and H3 hysteresis loops, suggesting the mesopores structure formed [65,66]. The BET specific surface areas (SBET) of g-C3N4, PCN, PCN@mCQDs-2, and PCN@hCQDs-2 were calculated to be 48, 88, 66, and 104 m2/g, respectively. The more obvious pore structure and large specific surface area of PCN may be explained as the three-dimensional porous network structure formed by the self-assembly process. While the significant changes in pore structure and specific surface area after mCQDs and hCQDs regulation also proved that the regulation effects of CQDs on the microstructure of as-prepared photocatalysts [49,67].

The XPS survey spectra in Fig. 3b demonstrate that all samples mainly consisted of C, N, and O elements. As shown in Fig. 3c and 3d, three fitting peaks at around 284.8, 286.2, and 288.3 eV represent C−C/C=C, C−OH, and N−C=N [68]. And Table S4 revealed the relative peak intensity percentages of C−OH (286.2 eV) increased after mCQDs regulation, which might provide evidence for the formation of hydrogen bonds between supramolecules and mCQDs in the process of supramolecular self-assembly. As depicted in Fig. 3e and 3f, N1s peaks of all samples could be divided into C−N=C (398.8 eV), N−(C)3 (400.0 eV), N−H (401.1 eV), and π-excitations (404.6 eV) [60,69]. The intensity of four characteristic peaks of all samples in the N1s spectra was similar, indicating that the load of CQDs has little effect on N containing chemical bonds [46].

The UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV-DRS) of all photocatalysts were depicted in Fig. 4 a. The absorption edge of g-C3N4 was observed to be approximately 443 nm, while that of PCN exhibits a blue shift, which indicated the loss of light absorption capacity [70]. After mCQDs and hCQDs regulation, the PCN@mCQDs-2 and PCN@hCQDs-2 broadened the spectral response range and showed strong absorption in the visible and infrared regions. And mCQDs and hCQDs showed similar absorption performance in the visible light band, while mCQDs have a higher light absorption capacity in the visible and near-infrared region. The optical band gaps of all samples were obtained according to the Tauc plot [71]. As illustrated in Fig. 4b, the bandgaps energy (Eg) of g-C3N4, PCN, PCN@mCQDs-2, and PCN@hCQDs-2 were calculated to be 2.77, 2.90, 2.83, and 2.85 eV, respectively.

The Mott-Schottky was measured to study the flat band and the semiconductor type of all samples. The positive slope of as-prepared photocatalysts in Fig. 4c demonstrated they were all n-type semiconductors [72]. The intersection points of the tangent of the C-2 curve and potential in the plot represented the flat band edge potential (Efb) of samples [56,73]. Hence, the Efb of g-C3N4, PCN, PCN@mCQDs-2, and PCN@hCQDs-2 were −0.88, −0.97, −1.34, and −0.94 V, respectively. Further, the conduct band (CB) edge potential (ECB) of photocatalysts could be calculated according to the formula that ECB (NHE, pH = 7) = Efb (SCE, pH = 7) + 0.24–0.2 [74]. So, the ECB of samples were obtained as −0.84, −0.92, −1.30, and −0.90 V, respectively. Based on the calculation formula of the valance band edge (EVB) potential (EVB = ECB + Eg), the EVB of samples above were obtained as 1.93, 1.98, 1.53, and 1.95 V, respectively. The EVB of PCN@mCQDs-2 and PCN@hCQDs-2 were further confirmed by XPS valence band spectra in Fig. S3. Then, the band positions of all samples concerning NHE at pH = 7.0 are provided in Fig. 4d. Previous researches have generally found the more positive CB position and constant VB position after loading CQDs because the electron accumulation effect of the CQDs [46,75–78]. However, the regulation of mCQDs significantly change the electronic band structure of as-prepared PCN@mCQDs-2.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of as-prepared photocatalysts were recorded under the excitation wavelength of 370 nm to evaluate the charge recombination rate. Fig. 5 a showed that the intensity of emission peak for PCN was much lower than that of g-C3N4. While PCN@mCQDs-2 has the lowest emission intensity, meaning the highest separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers [56]. Compared with PCN, the emission peak of PCN@mCQDs-2 and PCN@hCQDs-2 exhibited a red-shift from 458 to 469 nm and 464 nm, respectively, which was associated with the decreased bandgap energy after CQDs regulation [56]. In addition, the up-converted property of CQDs was also investigated by the PL spectra. As presented in Fig. S4, mCQDs and hCQDs could be excited by long-wavelength light (600–900 nm) with short-wavelength emission (395–700 nm). The above results implied that the mCQDs could expand the response range and improve the light absorption capacity [79,80].

The transient photocurrent responses of all samples were illustrated in Fig. 5b. The photocurrent intensity of PCN@mCQDs-2 was significantly higher than others, indicating PCN@mCQDs-2 held the fastest charge transfer rate of photogenerated carriers [81]. Fig. 5c describes the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) diagrams of as-prepared photocatalysts, the diameter of the arc radius of which have the following order: g-C3N4 > PCN > PCN@hCQDs-2 > PCN@mCQDs-2. The lower arc in the EIS Nyquist plot usually means lower charge-transfer resistance, so PCN@mCQDs-2 has the highest separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers [82]. The Ns-level time-resolved transient fluorescence decay spectroscopy was performed to further compare the transfer efficiency of carriers. The results were fitted by the exponential decay function, and three radiative lifetimes with different percentages were shown in Table S5. In particular,
τ 1
was related to free excitons recombination, and both
τ 2
and
τ 3
were related to non-radiative recombination, attributed to the de-trapping of charge carriers [83,84]. The average lifetime (
τ
average
) of g-C3N4, PCN, PCN@mCQDs-2, and PCN@hCQDs-2 were 9.65, 5.82, 3.04, and 3.65 ns, respectively [85]. The shortest lifetime of PCN@mCQDs-2 was contrary to the results of other characterizations above and the photocatalytic performance later. Hence, the enhanced separation and transfer efficiency of photogenerated carriers could be attributed to the close contact junction effect, as well as the emergence of non-radiative quenching pathways [81,86,87].

EPR spectra were conducted to monitor the unpaired electrons of as-prepared photocatalysts. The unpaired electrons, attributed to the delocalized electrons, could reflect the separation efficiency of the photogenerated carriers [35]. Originated from sp2-carbon atoms of the heptazine rings, the single Lorentzian lines were observed in PCN, PCN@mCQDs-2, and PCN@hCQDs-2 (Fig. S5) with a g value of 2.0048, 2.0045, and 2.0045, respectively [88]. The g value of PCN@mCQDs-2 and PCN@hCQDs-2 decreased after modification because of the increased delocalization and weak dipole–dipole electronic interaction [89]. Compared to other samples, PCN@mCQDs-2 exhibited the highest EPR spin intensity, suggesting the enhanced ability of electrons delocalization in the conjugated network and a higher concentration of unpaired electrons [49,56].

Based on the above analyses, mCQDs could involve the supramolecular self-assembly through the formation of hydrogen bonds and show promotion effects on the microstructure control and band structure adjustment of as-prepared PCN@mCQDs-2. PCN@mCQDs-2 also presented a broadened light response range, higher light absorption capacity, and enhanced separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers. So, PCN@mCQDs-2 has predictable high-efficiency photocatalytic activity.

The photocatalytic degradation of diclofenac sodium was exerted under simulated visible light. The kinetic data of all samples were fitted by the Langmuir-Hinshelwood pseudo-first-order kinetic model [90]. As presented in Fig. 6 a, the removal of DCF in the adsorption equilibrium experiment under dark conditions was negligible, indicating that all photocatalysts exhibited a weak adsorption effect on DCF. Almost no degradation of DCF was observed without photocatalyst, and DCF was also hardly degraded with the assistance of g-C3N4. In sharp contrast, PCN, PCN@mCQDs-x, and PCN@hCQDs-x presented a substantial improvement for the degradation of DCF. According to the calculation results shown in Fig. 6b, the PCN@mCQDs-2 exhibits the highest degradation activity, and the kinetic constant (kobs) of PCN@mCQDs-2 (kobs = 0.020 min−1) is about 25 and 4 times higher than that of g-C3N4 (kobs = 0.0008 min−1) and PCN (kobs = 0.0046 min−1), respectively. Fig. 6a and 6b also showed the content of mCQDs or hCQDs affects the photocatalytic degradation of DCF, presenting a trend of first increasing and then decreasing with the increase of the amount of CQDs. This phenomenon might be explained that an excess amount of CQDs may interfere with the formation of supramolecules and have a shield effect [46,68]. In addition, the photocatalytic degradation experiments of DCF under different long-wavelength light sources were conducted to verify the broadened response range and enhanced absorption capacity of PCN@mCQDs-2 and the up-conversion PL properties of mCQDs. As shown in Fig. S6a, PCN@mCQDs-2 presented improved photocatalytic performance under irradiation of blue light, blue-green light, and green light, and the kobs were about 74, 22, and 86 times higher than that of g-C3N4, respectively. Under orange light and red light irradiation, no degradation of DCF was observed over g-C3N4 in Fig. S6b, while PCN@mCQDs-2 showed weak degradation activity. This would imply that the long-wavelength light could be up-converted on mCQDs and subsequently absorbed by PCN@mCQDs-2. Due to high activity, PCN@mCQDs-2 was chosen as the optimum photocatalyst for further experimental investigations.

The initial pollutant concentration and the initial pH are crucial parameters of photocatalytic degradation, which were investigated and shown in Figs. S7a and S7b. With a fixed amount of photocatalysts added, the degradation rate of DCF decreased with the increase of the initial concentration. Unless otherwise stated, the initial concentration at 8 mg/L was chosen as the optimum dose for further experimental investigations. Fig. S7b shows that the degradation activity was significantly improved under alkaline conditions, which may be due to more superoxide anion production [46]. In addition, Fig. S7c revealed concomitant anions (20 mM Cl-, NO3 –, and SO4 2-) have minimal impact on the photocatalytic process, indicating PCN@mCQDs-2 has promising environmental application potential. As shown in Fig. S7d, the isoelectric point of PCN@mCQDs-2 was measured as about 4.12, close to the pKa of DCF (4.10). Therefore, the PCN@mCQDs-2 and DCF have the same charge when the pH > 4.12, which explained the poor adsorption effect [91]. The photocatalytic activity of PCN@mCQDs-2 was evaluated by removing a wide range of pollutants, including Diclofenac sodium (DCF), Naproxen (NPX), Acetaminophen (ACE), Bisphenol A (BPA), Ciprofloxacin (CIP), and Ofloxacin (OFL). The tests were conducted at the catalytic dosage of 0.1 g/L and pollutants concentration of 2 mg/L. As illustrated in Fig. 6c, PCN@mCQDs-2 exhibited significantly high degradation activity on tested PPCPs, and the kobs of DCF, NPX, ACE, BPA, CIP, and OFL (kobs = 0.041, 0.018, 0.031, 0.063, 0.039, and 0.081 min−1) were about 16, 5, 8, 39, 6, and 14 times higher than that of g-C3N4, respectively. The difference in the kobs of PCN@mCQDs-2 in removing all PPCPs mentioned above may be attributed to the difference in structure, which was related to the way and effect free radicals act. Overall, PCN@mCQDs-2 exhibited improved photocatalytic activity for all tested PPCPs.

As illustrated in Fig. 6d, with the addition of quenchers, the photocatalytic degradation activity of PCN@mCQDs-2 decreased 93%, 13%, 74%, 81%, and 70%, respectively. Obviously, photogenerated holes (h+) dominated the degradation of DCF, and all other free radicals except •OH also had a contribution. The 1:2:2:1 signal peaks and the multiple signal peaks were observed in Fig. 7 a and 7b, representing the •OH and •O2 − radicals signal, respectively. Obviously, PCN@mCQDs-2 presented negligible •OH radicals signals and weaker •O2 − radicals signal than g-C3N4, indicating •OH has not been produced and the •O2 − radical has little effect in the photocatalytic degradation of DCF over PCN@mCQDs-2. As shown in Fig. 7c, all as-prepared photocatalysis contained a 1:1:1 triplet signal of the TEMP-1O2 adduct under visible light irradiation, and PCN@mCQDs-2 revealed distinctly stronger signals than g-C3N4. Then, the triple signal would decrease because TEMPO would be reduced into TEMPOH when photogenerated electrons (e-) was present [73]. Compared to other photocatalysts, there was much e- in the photocatalytic system of PCN@mCQDs-2. The EPR results above are consistent with the scavenger-quenching experiments.

The liquid chromatography to quadrupole time-of-flight mass spectrometrywas conducted to identify the intermediates and explore degradation pathways of DCF. As shown in Table S6, Fig. 8 , and Fig. S8, a total of nine intermediates were detected. The degradation mechanism could be summarized into five reaction pathways, including (a) dehydration, (b) decarboxylation, (c) hydroxylation, (d) dechlorination, (e) formylation, and (f) dehydrogenation [92].

Considering that the amidation reaction occurs quickly under the deviation of the hydroxyl group from the N atom and a carboxyl group on DCF (m/z 296), intermediate (1) (m/z 278) was produced by the dehydration of DCF [92,93]. Consistent with previous research, intermediate (2) (m/z 250) was firstly produced from DCF after a loss of carboxyl group, which further transformed into intermediate (4) (m/z 215), (5) (m/z 266–1), and (6) (m/z 266–2) through the dechlorination, hydroxylation, and formylation reaction, respectively [93,94]. Then the dechlorination of intermediate (5) produced intermediate (8) (m/z 215). In addition, the DCF molecule was firstly hydroxylated into intermediate (3) (m/z 310–1) and further transformed into the intermediate (7) (m/z 310–2) with the formation of quinone imine structure due to hydrogen loss [46]. Similarly, intermediate (9) (m/z 266–3) with the formation of the quinone-like structure was derived from (6) and (7) through dehydrogenation and decarboxylation, and the former could be attributed to the dehydrogenation after hydroxylation at C13 of DCF [94].

Based on the results and discussions above, the photocatalytic processes in the PCN@mCQDs-2 system were proposed and illustrated in Fig. 9 . Under irradiation, PCN@mCQDs-2 would be excited and form the photogenerated electron-hole pairs after absorbing the light with < 438 nm wavelength, facilitated by the reflection and scattering effects in the hollow tubular structure. With superior optical properties and electronic transfer performance, mCQDs acted as a photosensitizer and an electron mediator in this process [45,50]. On the one hand, the long-wavelength light could be up-converted on mCQDs and subsequently absorbed by PCN@mCQDs-2. On the other hand, e- in the CB of PCN@mCQDs-2 could efficiently transfer to mCQDs, promoting the separation efficiency of carriers. And the shortened stacking distance and the emergence of non-radiative quenching pathways of PCN@mCQDs-2 also restrain the recombination of electron-hole pairs. Then, separated e- could produce ·O2 −, 1O2, and ·OH, while h+ would gather on the VB of PCN and destroy the absorbed DCF molecule directly rather than generate ·OH [51,75,76]. The excellent hollow tubular structure of PCN@mCQDs-2 could boost interfacial charge transfer and provide abundant active sites on which DCF would be absorbed and removed.

The acute toxicity tests of the photocatalytic degradation products of DCF were conducted as shown in Fig. 10 a. Due to the degradation and mineralization of DCF, the inhibition ratio of luminescent bacteria (Photobacterium phoshoreum T3 spp) showed an overall downward trend. And the reusability of the PCN@mCQDs-2 for the degradation of DCF was investigated and demonstrated in Fig. 10b. In five successive cycles, the degradation efficiencies were relatively stable. Hence, PCN@mCQDs-2 has high stability and reusability.

A novel hollow tubular PCN@mCQDs-2 was successfully synthesized by facile polymerization using self-assembly melamine-cyanuric acid supermolecules regulated by carbon quantum dots as precursors. The results of characterization indicated that mCQDs involved in the process of supramolecular self-assembly through the formation of hydrogen bonds between surface groups of mCQDs and supramolecules. The introduction of mCQDs significantly affected the synthesis process and showed regulation effects on the microstructure control, band structure adjustment, and performance promotion of PCN@mCQDs-2, which presented excellent hollow tubular structure, increased crystallinity, shortened stacking distance, improved surface areas, and enhanced pore structure. Meantime, due to the unique up-converted PL properties and outstanding electron transport performance of mCQDs, as-prepared PCN@mCQDs-2 has a broadened light response range, higher light absorption capacity, and enhanced separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers. Benefited from the multiple advantages, PCN@mCQDs-2 exhibits high photocatalytic performance for several PPCPs, among which PCN@mCQDs-2 has ∼25 times higher activity for DCF degradation than pure g-C3N4. Scavenger-quenching experiments and electron spin resonance technique revealed that photogenerated holes dominated the degradation of DCF, while hydroxyl radical showed a negligible contribution. Based on the identification of intermediates, the photocatalytic DCF degradation pathways were proposed. Experimental results reflected that PCN@mCQDs-2 showed photocatalytic activity even under long-wavelength light irradiation and provided a very desirable performance in various water quality parameter tests. Further research indicated that PCN@mCQDs-2 has high toxicity reduction properties, mineralization ability, and reusability.
Hence, our results demonstrate that PCN@mCQDs-2 is a promising photocatalyst in the removal of diclofenac. And we provide a strategy for the fabrication of enhanced g-C3N4 for environment protection.
